# CSCE 670 — Information Storage and Retrieval (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 670.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 670 Syllabus
Section 600 (46627)
Information Storage and Retrieval
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 1:50PM
End Time: 2:40PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  HRBB 113
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Yu Zhang
Email:  yuzhang@tamu.edu
Office Location:  PETR 222
Phone:  (217) 954-3457
Office Hours
Mondays 3:30 – 4:30 PM
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Information Storage and Retrieval. (3-0). Credit 3. Representation, storage, and
access to very large multimedia document collections; fundamental data structures
and algorithms of information storage and retrieval systems; techniques to design
and evaluate complete retrieval systems, including cover of algorithms for indexing,
compressing, and querying very large collections. Prerequisites: CSCE 310 or CSCE
603 or approval of instructor; graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 310 or CSCE 603 or approval of instructor;
graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to web search, including
topics like text indexing, retrieval models, evaluation, Web crawling, link-based
algorithms like PageRank, and learning to rank.
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to recommender
systems, including topics like collaborative filtering, matrix factorization,
recommender system evaluation, and implicit recommendation.
• Design, implement, and evaluate the core algorithms underlying a fully functional
web search system and recommendation system.
• Identify the salient features and apply recent research results in web search and
recommender systems, including topics such as adversarial information retrieval and
neural models of retrieval and recommendation.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Optional
Mining of Massive Datasets
Authors:  Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, and Jeffrey D. Ullman
Publication Date:  2014
Notes:
Available online:  http://www.mmds.org/
This material Is: Optional
Introduction to Information Retrieval
Authors:  Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schutze
Publication Date:  2009
Notes:
Available online:  https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
The course grading scale is:
A : >=90%
B : >=80% and < 90%
C : >=70% and < 80%
D : >=60% and < 70%
F : < 60%
[11.0pt bold] Homework 30% : We will have five programming assignments
(2%+7%+7%+7%+7%). All of them will be in Python. The first assignment is an
introductory one designed to help you get familiar with Jupyter Notebook and the
Python operations commonly used in this course.
[11.0pt bold] Quizzes 20% : We will have four brief in-class quizzes (5%+5%+5%+5%).
[11.0pt bold] Final 30% : We will hold a comprehensive final exam from 3:30 - 5:30 PM on May 4
in HRBB 113.
[11.0pt bold] Group Project 20% : Students will work in teams of 3 or 4 to engage in a mini-
research project.
Homework collaboration policy : Homeworks are individual assignments. That is, you
should write your own code, by yourself. However, we do encourage you to talk
amongst yourselves about approaches and methods. As an example, it is
appropriate to sit in a group with no laptops to talk through the problems. It is
inappropriate, however, to pair program, share code, or work through specific
problems in code together. You must acknowledge all help in your homework
submission.
Regrade policy : Once you receive your graded assignment (e.g., a homework
assignment), you have seven days to request a regrade in writing. After seven days,
there will be no regrades. You must give the instructor a written explanation of your
regrade request. We reserve the right to regrade the entire assignment.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Homework late day policy : All homeworks are due by 11:59pm on the due date.
Each student gets five late days total. A late day is an indivisible 24-hour unit. For
example, if the due date is 11:59pm on Monday, and you submit at 12:01am
Tuesday, that is 1 late day. Once you use up the five late days, a late assignment will
receive a grade of 0.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
(this is a rough schedule for the course; subject to change)
Week 1: Course Overview; Boolean Retrieval
Week 2: TF-IDF; Vector Space Model; BM25
Week 3: Link Analysis
Week 4: Evaluation of Search Engines
Week 5: Learning to Rank
Week 6: Recommender Systems (Content-Based; Collaborative Filtering)
Week 7: Recommender Systems (Matrix Factorization; Bayesian Personalized
Ranking)
Week 8: word2vec; Neural Ranking
Week 9: Spring Break
Week 10: Transformer; BERT-Based Ranking
Week 11: Neural Collaborative Filtering; Sequential Recommendation
Week 12: Large Language Models Basics
Week 13: Large Language Models with Search Engines
Week 14: Large Language Models for Ranking and Recommendation
Week 15: Wrap-up; Project Presentations
Important Dates
Homeworks
Homework 0: due Jan 26
Homework 1: due Feb 9
Homework 2: due Mar 2
Homework 3: due Mar 30
Homework 4: due Apr 20
Quizzes
Quiz 1: in the Feb 6 class
Quiz 2: in the Feb 27 class
Quiz 3: in the Mar 25 class
Quiz 4: in the Apr 15 class
Group Project
Proposal: due Mar 7
Presentation: in the Apr 20, Apr 22, Apr 24, and Apr 27 classes
Report: due May 2
Final: May 4
[16.0pt] AI Statement
AI policy : In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on
or derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in
the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the
response. AI tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that
the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program
logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code,
even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in
particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The
submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.

doc-5ab8c9dba497f51ad0f36890061446b8.content:
# CSCE 605 — Compiler Design (Fall 2025)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 605.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 605 Syllabus
Section 600 (62077)
Compiler Design
Fall 2025 - College Station
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Khanh Nguyen
Email:  khanhtn@tamu.edu
Office:  PETR 229
Office Hours
W 2:00 - 3:00 PM or by appointment (via Google Calendar)
in-person only
[16.0pt] Course Description
Compiler Design. (3-0). Credit 3. Advanced topics in compiler writing; parser
generators and compiler-compilers; dynamic storage and scope resolution; data
flow analysis and code optimization. Prerequisite: CSCE 434.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 434.
This course is a continuation of a Programming Languages course; hence, previous
knowledge is expected. Sufficient programming experience and background are
required.
Technical Skills Expectations:
Students should be familiar with installing libraries/packages, configuring
environments, executing the compilers, and running compiled programs (with or
without an IDE). Familiarity with the Linux environment is needed. Experience with
Git is required.
Prerequisites Waiver:
Graduate students who are unsure about prerequisites should discuss with the
instructor.
Course Auditing:
Auditing a course must go through a formal approval process per student rules.
This is often for very rare extenuating scenarios, i.e., the reason cannot be to
prepare to take the class next semester, or to effectively retake a course without
being enrolled.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the student will be able to:
Understand the translation process from user code to machine code.
Describe major components of a typical modern compiler and their function.
Apply parsing techniques such as LL(1), SLR(1), and LR(1).
Design and implement sound dataflow analyses.
Understand and implement code optimization techniques.
Develop a front end and a back end of an optimizing compiler that can
generate executable machine code from source code.
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
[Dragon] Compilers: Principles, Techniques, & Tools (aka the Dragon book)
Authors:  Alfred Aho, Monica Lam, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey Ullman
Publication Date:  2006
Edition:  2nd
This material Is: Required
[EaC] Engineering: A Compiler
Authors:  Keith Cooper, Linda Torczon
Publication Date:  2011
Edition:  2nd
URL for Resource:  https://go.oreilly.com/TAMU/library/view/-/9780080916613
Notes:
The 2nd edition is no longer in print. The new, 3rd edition (ISBN 9780128154120) is
available in the bookstore. Using either edition will be fine for readings. Assigned
readings are based on 2nd edition.
This material Is: Required
[PPA] Principles of Program Analysis
Authors:  Fleming Nielson, Hanne Riis Nielson, Chris Hankin
Publication Date:  1999
Edition:  2nd
URL for Resource:  https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-662-03811-6
Notes:
We will use Chapters 1 and 2
This material Is: Required
Google Drive
Notes:
Students must use TAMU credentials to access the shared drive.  Allow 24 hours
after registering for the class to access this shared drive.
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
The course grade is based on the following components:
Quizzes: 4 online quizzes: 15% of grade. Online quizzes are conducted online
using Respondus Lockdown Browser. All quizzes are open-note only. Each will
carry a different weight.
Exams: 1 midterm exam - 20% of grade, and 1 non-cumulative final exam -
25% of grade). Exams areopen book, open note, closed internet, and closed
neighbor. Exams are conducted in-person.
Project: 40% of grade. This will be a semester-long project building a
complete optimizing compiler with multiple parts, with later components
depending on previous ones.
Graduate students are subject to additional requirements as follows:
Extra and/or different questions on quizzes, midterm, and final exams.
Additional features/optimizations required in project assignments.
Passing additional test cases when turning in project assignments to earn full
credit.
[12.9pt] Course Grading Scale:  F < 48%  ≤  D < 58%  ≤  C < 72%  ≤  B < 86%  ≤  A
Cutpoints are non-negotiable. Furthermore, people are cutpoints; whole-
numbered percentages are not. In other words, the cutpoint for a particular
grade is the lowest-scoring person who got assigned that grade.
The grade assigned is not on a curve. In this course, no curving will happen.
There will be no extra-credit assignments.
The grades may be audited randomly at any point in the course. This may
result in increased/decreased scores for released grades. Affected students
will be notified.
[12.9pt] Class Participation:
This course strictly enforces the University’s Attendance Policy, which states
The University views class attendance and participation as an
individual student responsibility.
Students are expected to attend class and to complete all
assignments.
No credit will be awarded for class attendance. If students miss class, it is their
responsibility to catch up with the materials and announcements. The course staff
will not repeat announcements that were made in class.
Attendance Certification:
As required by Federal aid regulations, the University requires an attendance
certification of each student having begun engagement in an academically related
activity. This course uses submissions of assignments, quizzes, homeworks as
evidence to satisfy the required documentation. The attendance certification is due
approximately at the end of the 3rd week. Students are hereby notified that by the
due date - specifically  09/12/2025 5:00 PM , if students have zero submissions, they
will not be certified of this course’s attendance. There will be no warning or
communication should a student be at risk of not being certified. Failure of this
certification might negatively impact students’ financial aid.
[12.9pt] Regrading:
Regrading can be requested for any graded materials. The guidelines are:
The request must be made on Gradescope and within a specified window.
Outside of this window, no grading requests will be accepted. No requests via
email will be accepted. No exceptions will be made.
The request must include a reasonable justification and/or documentation.
Non-code submissions (e.g., writing assignments, exams): If the handwriting is
not legible, what the graders guess is final, and the regrade request will be
denied.
The following kinds of submissions will get 0 without grading, and regrading
will be denied:
Disorganized, messy submissions.
Wrong submissions or submissions that did not follow the instructions
(e.g., submitting different file types, not matching the assignment
outline with the Gradescope submission, using banned libraries).
Uncompilable programming assignments on Gradescope. “It works on
my machine” argument will be ignored.
[12.9pt] Makeup Exam Policy
Unless due to excused absences, no makeup exam will be given. If an excused
absence is expected, students should contact the instructor as soon as possible
(with documentation). Makeup exams will be different from the original exams.
If a student misses an exam due to an excused absence, the student must take the
makeup exam on the designated date and time:
Midterm: Monday, 10/6/25 at 7:00 PM in PETR 226
Final: Monday, 12/15/25 at 7:00 PM in PETR 226
Discuss with the instructor directly if rescheduling is required.
[12.9pt] Incomplete Grade Policy
Per Student Rule 10, the instructor shall give an incomplete grade only in cases due
to an authorized absence or other causes beyond the control of the student, such
as illness or personal emergency. No exceptions will be made. Students are
required to furnish appropriate documentation. Under no circumstances can the “I”
grade be given to a student who wishes to:
Do additional work after the course deadline to improve a grade
Redo the coursework in a subsequent semester to improve the current grade
Awarding an incomplete is always at the discretion of the instructor.
[12.9pt] Deviations from the grading policy
The grade for individual assignments and the course is assigned to each student
based on concrete, quantifiable, objectively evaluated submissions and exams.
Grade is not given to students. Hence, in the interest of fairness and consistency,
requests for grade increases that are inconsistent with the stated grading scale are
denied.
Here is an incomplete list of hypothetical requests:
(I feel) I do not deserve [the grade/score] because of [reason(s)]
(I feel) The (grading/course) rubric is unbalanced due to [reason(s)]
I spend [huge number]% of my time on X which accounts for only [small
number]% of the grade, [elaborate on how the grading should be here]
I do very well on other assignments, but I panicked during tests
With the grade lower than . . . , I will be [insert a consequence here]
I worked very hard for the class, but because of [reason(s)] I failed
I have too many other responsibilities.
I am really close to getting a . . .
I am willing to do extra work
I have a good GPA so far
I have never failed a class before.
I have never taken a course in this area before
I have never gotten a grade as low as . . . before
It doesn’t hurt to ask
I am about to graduate.
This course is too hard
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
[12.9pt] Submission of Assignments and Grade Posting:
All assignments must be turned in electronically on Gradescope. Email
submissions will not be accepted. Emails with submission attachments will be
deleted without response. No exceptions will be made.
Students are advised not to use Gradescope as a debugging tool, as the
autograder might be buggy. Students, therefore, should focus on debugging
locally.
Grade posting: The score shown in Gradescope will be imported into Canvas
as-is after the deadline (and regrading).
[12.9pt] Assignment Deadlines and Late Penalty:
All assignments have established deadlines. Submitting a deliverable after the
deadline is late work. Late submission is allowed but will incur a late penalty. No late
work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved
absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the grace
period.
Should deadline discrepancies arise between the syllabus, assignment
description PDF, and Gradescope: the deadline on Gradescope takes
precedence and is the official deadline.
Project assignments: Unless a project assignment is restricted as "hard-
deadline", late submission is allowed with a 39-hour grace period. No late
work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved
absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the
grace period.
Late submission will be graded as if it were on time, and then a late penalty
will be deducted (out of full credit). Late penalty will be calculated as follows:
Let h be the whole number of hours late (rounding up), the penalty is
h²/24 + h. That is, a 12-hour-late assignment will receive -18% (out of
100% — full credit) as a penalty; 1 day late, -48%. At 36 hours late, the
most one can score in an assignment is 10. At 39 hours late, the
assignment receives no credit.
See the graph below:
Late penalty is waived in cases of university-approved absences
Excused absences: Students are required to furnish documentation for
an excused absence by default. When possible, notify the course staff
of excused absences before the deadline - not after. Refer to Student
Rule 7 for information about excused absences. This course will strictly
enforce Rule 7.
Computer crashes, malfunctions, and catastrophic loss of files are not valid
excuses for not submitting work on time (and thus request for the waiver of
the late penalty). Students are advised to turn in assignments early to avoid
any issues due to emergencies.
Students are responsible for making sure submissions are successfully
submitted on Gradescope. Unless due to global, public outages of
Gradescope, networking connectivity problems are not excuses for missing or
late Gradescope submissions.
Technology issues are not an excuse for missing a deadline. Make sure to
prepare the environments for assignments well in advance of deadlines, and
reach out to course staff early for help.
Students are responsible for ensuring submissions are successfully
submitted on Gradescope.
The instructor reserves the right to define makeup work for an excused absence,
which is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
[12.9pt] Topics
Dragon = Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman
EaC = Cooper, Torczon
PPA = Nielson, Nielson, Hankin
[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
[12.9pt] Project Logistics
The language used for the project is Java. No other language is allowed.
For the first 2 project assignments, students must work individually.
From the third project assignment till the last (6th), students must work in
pairs, from the same section.
Students may choose to work individually, but should be aware that the
project’s requirement is not reduced proportionally.
Students may break from the pair only after the third project assignment, but
must find themselves a new partner for the last three project assignments.
After the fourth assignment, if the pair structure still does not work for a
student, that student goes solo.
Public testcases requirement:
Each student must contribute at least 1 test to the set of public testcases for each
project assignment to a shared GitHub repository. Each test is required to satisfy 3
requirements:
1. Unique: the test must be reasonably different from existing tests.
2. On time: the test must be committed and pushed to the shared repository 72
hours before the assignment deadline. The cut-off for test submission is 24
hours before the assignment deadline.
3. Usable: the test must have no inconsistency. It is the author’s responsibility to
fix issues by the cut-off.
5% of full credit penalty each if the test is duplicated, late, or inconsistent; the
penalty can be applied at any time after the deadline.
No test submission penalty: 17% of full credit
Project assignments grading:
Each front-end project assignment (PA 1-3) grade:
20%: passing 80% of public tests
80%: passing 100% of private tests
Back-end project assignments (PA 4-6) will be graded as a whole at the end of
the semester. Further details of the grading are in the project description.
[12.9pt] Professionalism and Respect
Students are expected to treat the instructor and all other participants in the
course with courtesy and respect — refer to Student Rule 21. Comments to others
should be factual, constructive, and free from harassment. Unprofessional or
disrespectful conduct will not be tolerated. Warnings will not be given. Students will
need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive ways within the course.
Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions
as specified within the Student Rules.
[12.9pt] Recording Statement
Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student
has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording.
This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any
recording being done. Students with permission to record classes are not
permitted to redistribute audio or video recordings of statements or comments
from the course to other individuals without the express permission of the faculty
member and of any students who are recorded.
[12.9pt] Course Material Policy
Materials used in this course are subject to copyright. Students are not allowed to
reproduce, distribute, or post any lecture materials, assignments, exams, solutions,
etc., publicly. Students may take notes and make copies of course materials for
personal use. Students may not post course materials on commercial websites
such as Course Hero and Chegg. Doing so is a copyright violation and, in some
cases, may also be an academic integrity violation that will be dealt with accordingly.
[12.9pt] Campuswire
Campuswire will be the main communication and Q&A platform, not Canvas. The
class link and code to join will be provided in the first class meeting. If you drop the
class, please leave Campuswire. Interfering with or disrupting Campuswire will be
reported and result in disciplinary actions according to Student Rules.
Unless regarding personal matters (e.g., grade), questions are to be posted
on Campuswire first, for the benefit of other students. Students who wish to
discuss personal matters should see the course staff during office hours.
Students are encouraged to participate in the forum and help answer
questions. Campuswire is mainly made for students by students.
Students must never post codes or solutions on Campuswire for any reason.
If asking for implementation clarification/guide, describe what has been done
in pseudocode. Posts with codes/solutions will be deleted, and the poster
might be banned from Campuswire.
All posts to Campuswire must be public (visible to everyone) and un-
anonymous. Private posts or messages to the course staff will be deleted.
The order of getting answers is: Campuswire or in class, or office hours.
Emailing should be last.
Students must not treat Campuswire as a de-facto email system (e.g., make a
post visible to TA and instructor only and discuss code/solution of the
student). Those who violate will be banned from Campuswire.
The course staff will not check Campuswire during non-working hours, e.g.,
weekends and late hours.
In the past, students tended to wait until the due date and then panic and ask
questions. This is ineffective and discouraged. To enforce better behavior, the
course staff will not check Campuswire during the last 6 hours before the
deadline.
[12.9pt] Respondus Lockdown Browser
Some assessments will be conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser
with Monitor - and will be referred to as quizzes. There is no cost to students to use
the software. Students are required to have a webcam, which may be built into the
computer or may be an external camera. The software is available for Mac, PC, and
Chromebooks. It is important to test LockDown Browser and the webcam before
using the application in a quiz. There will be an opportunity for students to practice
and set up the environment. Students must ensure that they have a functional
environment during the course to take quizzes. Unless due to extended excused
absences, no makeup is possible.
Note: the “Available Until” date and time is a hard limit, triggering a submission
automatically, and overriding any remaining time on the timer. Each quiz is not
longer than 1 hour.
University Library (Evans Annex) Tech Bar: Any Texas A&M student with an
active NetID can check out electronics such as Chromebooks, chargers, and
webcams from the Tech Bar for free. Equipment reservations are available up
to seven days in advance. Walkups are also possible. For Chromebooks, use
https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/chromebooksAnnex ; for accessories, use
https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/computeraccessoriesAnnex
Respondus should be available for use on these Chromebooks as a Chrome
extension.
Alternatively, students may be able to take Respondus Lockdown Browser
exams in select computer labs on campus. See TAMU—Open Access Lab
(OAL) Computer Workstation Locations ( http://tx.ag/TAMUoal ) for more
information.
[12.9pt] Course Announcements
Aside from Campuswire posts, the instructor will make class announcements via a
mailing list:  csce-compiler-fall2025@lists.tamu.edu  - not Canvas. Students should
make sure they can receive emails from the mailing list. All emails to the mailing list
are assumed to be read after 12 hours. Allow 24 hours after registering for the class
for the roster to be synchronized with the mailing list. Past emails may be found on
the Google Group page.
How to succeed in this course (or any course):
Here are a few tips:
Attend class meetings regularly.
Take notes during lectures.
Read assigned readings before the lecture and your notes after class. Read
the assigned readings again after class.
Ask questions and participate in discussions in class and on Campuswire.
Campuswire is FYBY - For You and By You.
Practice, practice, practice. For each credit hour, you are expected to spend 3
hours per week outside of class. Allow sufficient time to absorb the materials.
Study for exams. Group study/review for exams is allowed. Students in the
past have found group review sessions and crowd-sourcing practice tests and
review sheets helpful.
Complete assignments on time.
Visit the course staff during office hours to get questions answered.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
[12.9pt] Plagiarism
Plagiarism is one of the worst academic sins, for the plagiarist destroys trust among
colleagues and the purpose of learning. Plagiarism is commonly defined as passing
off as one’s own ideas, words, writings, code, etc., which belong to another person.
By this definition, in this course, a student commits plagiarism if copying the work
elsewhere, even with the permission of the author, and turns it in as one’s own.
The instructor encourages collaboration and the exchange of ideas among
students. Collaboration is important for facilitating learning, and peers often can be
a great resource. However, all assignments in this course are individual efforts. All
assignments must be fully completed by only the student to whom they are
assigned, without unauthorized aid. Any academic dishonesty will not be tolerated
and will be severely dealt with. Consequences include a zero grade for the
activity/assignment, course failure, and dismissal from the university, among others.
Refer to the Academic Integrity Statement and Policy below. All involved parties will
be penalized equally.
Specifically, students may discuss the assignments at a high level (e.g.,
concepts, approaches) with friends but absolutely no actual solution sharing
(voluntarily or involuntarily) is allowed.
Code: Students are not allowed to use code found elsewhere (e.g., Google
searches, online repositories, past offerings of the course). The use of a
private code repository, with a detailed commit history, is recommended and
may be requested at any time to answer authorship inquiries. Students also
must not attempt to circumvent the objective of an assignment (e.g., using a
banned library or a function to bypass steps outlined in the assignment)
Non-code: The write-up of homeworks must be done individually and must
not be shared in any way or form.
No full or partial deliverables are allowed to be knowingly or unknowingly
released in the public domain even after the deadlines and after the course is
over. Students are solely responsible for securing all deliverables away from
the public.
Group review sessions for exams are allowed and encouraged.
Posting assignments to or copying solutions from unauthorized sources is
academic misconduct.
There have been past incidents where students used code from Chegg or
similar tutoring websites; the use of such resources is not allowed.
Complicity
Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to
help another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also
constitutes academic dishonesty and carries the same punishment as
cheating and plagiarism.
In other words, if providing a solution to another student, even if that student
does not turn it in for credit, the provider has committed an act of academic
dishonesty.
Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the assignments have been
graded. For example, a prior assignment may be flagged when a new detector
becomes available. For these reasons, you should treat your assignment grades as
provisional. Any prior submission that is later found to constitute a violation will be
reported to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO).
[12.9pt] Use of Generative AI
The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its
capabilities. This is allowed.
Code: Students may use AI tools and submit AI-generated code, or the
derivation from such code for the project assignments, as long as this use is
properly documented in the code/comments and the final project report.
Writing: All non-code submissions (e.g., reports) must be generated by
students themselves. Students must not have another entity write, produce,
or generate any portion of a submission for them.

# CSCE 638 — Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 638.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 638 Syllabus
Section 600 (54988)
Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MW
Start Time: 4:10PM
End Time: 5:25PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  HRBB 124
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Kuan-Hao Huang
Email:  khhuang@tamu.edu
Office Location:  PETR 219
Phone:  (979) 458-2205
Office Hours
[13.0pt] Wednesday 2-3pm
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques. (3-0). Credit 3. Focus
on teaching Natural Language Processing (NLP) fundamentals including language
models, automatic syntactic processing and semantic understanding; introduction
to major NLP applications including information extraction, machine translation,
text summarization, dialogue systems and sentiment analysis.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Identify and explain the key concepts and models the fundamental concepts
and models for natural language processing.
Be aware of recent trends in natural language processing research.
Apply established techniques and propose practical methods and solutions
to tackle challenges in natural language processing.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Recommended
Speech and Language Processing. An Introduction to
Natural Language Processing, Computational Linguistics,
and Speech Recognition
ISBN:  9791221476842
Authors:  Daniel Jurafsky, James H. Martin
Publication Date:  2023-01-01
